questions.question: Music from a range of African traditions. What is this?
questions.hint: Layered percussion, interlocking rhythms and call and response.
questions.explanation: Music from a range of African traditions.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of African music?

answers.text: The story is mainly sung on stage with costumes, scenery and an orchestra.
Layered percussion and repeated rhythmic patterns are common course features.
Call and response, singing and clapping may be heard.
Interlocking or overlapping rhythms and improvisation may be used.

questions.hint: A staged drama where almost every word is sung.
questions.explanation: The statement “The story is mainly sung on stage with costumes, scenery and an orchestra” describes Opera, not African music. Music from a range of African traditions.

questions.question: Common features include basso continuo, contrapuntal texture, ornaments, sequences, terraced dynamics and instruments such as harpsichord and organ. What is this?
questions.hint: Harpsichord, basso continuo and terraced dynamics.
questions.explanation: Common features include basso continuo, contrapuntal texture, ornaments, sequences, terraced dynamics and instruments such as harpsichord and organ.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Baroque?

answers.text: Basso continuo and contrapuntal textures are common.
Rubato, chromatic harmony and a greatly expanded orchestra are common features.
Ornaments, sequences and terraced dynamics are often used.
Harpsichord and organ are strongly associated with the style.

questions.hint: Rubato, sweeping melodies and a greatly expanded orchestra.
questions.explanation: The statement “Rubato, chromatic harmony and a greatly expanded orchestra are common features” describes Romantic, not Baroque. Common features include basso continuo, contrapuntal texture, ornaments, sequences, terraced dynamics and instruments such as harpsichord and organ.

questions.question: A piece for solo instrument and orchestra. What is this?
questions.hint: A featured soloist faces an orchestra.
questions.explanation: A piece for solo instrument and orchestra.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Concerto?

answers.text: A solo instrument is contrasted with an orchestra.
The soloist has a prominent role.
A staged story is mainly sung with acting, costumes and scenery.
It is usually divided into several movements.

questions.hint: A staged drama where almost every word is sung.
questions.explanation: The statement “A staged story is mainly sung with acting, costumes and scenery” describes Opera, not Concerto. A piece for solo instrument and orchestra.

questions.question: A Scottish Gaelic vocal style, also called puirt-a-beul, using rhythmic Gaelic or nonsense syllables. What is this?
questions.hint: Dance rhythms sung with Gaelic or nonsense syllables.
questions.explanation: A Scottish Gaelic vocal style, also called puirt-a-beul, using rhythmic Gaelic or nonsense syllables.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Mouth music?

answers.text: It is a Scottish Gaelic vocal style also called puirt-a-beul.
Rhythmic Gaelic or nonsense syllables are sung.
It is usually unaccompanied and was used for dancing.
A big band performs riffs, swung rhythms and sections of brass and saxophones.

questions.hint: Big-band jazz with a long-short rhythmic feel.
questions.explanation: The statement “A big band performs riffs, swung rhythms and sections of brass and saxophones” describes Swing, not Mouth music. A Scottish Gaelic vocal style, also called puirt-a-beul, using rhythmic Gaelic or nonsense syllables.

questions.question: A stage drama in which most or all of the story is sung. What is this?
questions.hint: A staged drama where almost every word is sung.
questions.explanation: A stage drama in which most or all of the story is sung.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Opera?

answers.text: A jazz singer improvises with nonsense syllables instead of normal lyrics.
It is a staged drama in which the story is mainly sung.
Soloists, chorus and an orchestra are used.
Acting, costumes, scenery and theatrical movement are included.

questions.hint: Improvised jazz vocals using nonsense syllables.
questions.explanation: The statement “A jazz singer improvises with nonsense syllables instead of normal lyrics” describes Scat singing, not Opera. A stage drama in which most or all of the story is sung.

questions.question: It combines a syncopated melody with a steady bass-and-chord, or vamp-like, accompaniment. What is this?
questions.hint: Syncopated right hand over steady bass-and-chord piano.
questions.explanation: It combines a syncopated melody with a steady bass-and-chord, or vamp-like, accompaniment.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Ragtime?

answers.text: A syncopated melody is often played on piano.
It is a Jamaican popular style with a relaxed groove and off-beat guitar chords.
The accompaniment keeps a steady bass-and-chord pattern.
The left hand often alternates bass notes and chords.

questions.hint: Deep bass and clipped chords on the off-beats.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a Jamaican popular style with a relaxed groove and off-beat guitar chords” describes Reggae, not Ragtime. It combines a syncopated melody with a steady bass-and-chord, or vamp-like, accompaniment.

questions.question: Rhyming words spoken in time with a beat. What is this?
questions.hint: Rhyming words spoken rhythmically over a groove.
questions.explanation: Rhyming words spoken in time with a beat.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Rapping?

answers.text: Rhyming or rhythmically organised words are spoken.
The words are delivered in time to a beat.
The full dramatic story is mainly sung on stage with an orchestra.
Repeated grooves, samples and backing tracks may be used.

questions.hint: A staged drama where almost every word is sung.
questions.explanation: The statement “The full dramatic story is mainly sung on stage with an orchestra” describes Opera, not Rapping. Rhyming words spoken in time with a beat.

questions.question: A relaxed, steady groove with a strong low bass line and short guitar or keyboard chords on off-beats, often stressing beats 2 and 4. What is this?
questions.hint: Deep bass and clipped chords on the off-beats.
questions.explanation: A relaxed, steady groove with a strong low bass line and short guitar or keyboard chords on off-beats, often stressing beats 2 and 4.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Reggae?

answers.text: It has a relaxed, steady groove and a prominent low bass line.
Short staccato guitar or keyboard chords fall on the off-beats.
A piano plays a syncopated melody over alternating bass notes and chords.

questions.hint: Syncopated right hand over steady bass-and-chord piano.
questions.explanation: The statement “A piano plays a syncopated melody over alternating bass notes and chords” describes Ragtime, not Reggae. A relaxed, steady groove with a strong low bass line and short guitar or keyboard chords on off-beats, often stressing beats 2 and 4.

questions.question: Common features include expressive melody, rubato, chromatic harmony, wide dynamic range, programmatic ideas and an expanded orchestra. What is this?
questions.hint: Rubato, sweeping melodies and a greatly expanded orchestra.
questions.explanation: Common features include expressive melody, rubato, chromatic harmony, wide dynamic range, programmatic ideas and an expanded orchestra.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Romantic?

answers.text: Basso continuo, terraced dynamics and harpsichord are central features.
Expressive melodies, rubato and chromatic harmony are common.
A wide dynamic range and programmatic ideas may be used.
The orchestra is larger and includes a wider range of instruments.

questions.hint: Harpsichord, basso continuo and terraced dynamics.
questions.explanation: The statement “Basso continuo, terraced dynamics and harpsichord are central features” describes Baroque, not Romantic. Common features include expressive melody, rubato, chromatic harmony, wide dynamic range, programmatic ideas and an expanded orchestra.

questions.question: A Scottish narrative song sung in Scots. What is this?
questions.hint: A story sung in Scots, often verse by verse.
questions.explanation: A Scottish narrative song sung in Scots.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Scots ballad?

answers.text: It is a Scottish narrative song sung in Scots.
It is a staged drama with acting, costumes, scenery and an orchestra.
It tells a story and is often strophic.
It may be sung solo with simple accompaniment or unaccompanied.

questions.hint: A staged drama where almost every word is sung.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a staged drama with acting, costumes, scenery and an orchestra” describes Opera, not Scots ballad. A Scottish narrative song sung in Scots.

questions.question: A danceable swung rhythm with riffs and call and response, played by trumpets, trombones, saxophones and a rhythm section. What is this?
questions.hint: Big-band jazz with a long-short rhythmic feel.
questions.explanation: A danceable swung rhythm with riffs and call and response, played by trumpets, trombones, saxophones and a rhythm section.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Swing?

answers.text: A big band often performs the style.
It uses a swung rhythmic feel, dance pulse, riffs and call and response.
It is an unaccompanied Gaelic vocal style using rhythmic nonsense syllables.
Trumpet, trombone and saxophone sections play with a rhythm section.

questions.hint: Dance rhythms sung with Gaelic or nonsense syllables.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is an unaccompanied Gaelic vocal style using rhythmic nonsense syllables” describes Mouth music, not Swing. A danceable swung rhythm with riffs and call and response, played by trumpets, trombones, saxophones and a rhythm section.

questions.question: The notes of a chord are played one after another, not together. What is this?

answers.text: Chord progressions - chords I, IV and V in major keys

questions.hint: A chord's notes flow one after another.
questions.explanation: The notes of a chord are played one after another, not together.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Broken chord / arpeggio?

answers.text: The notes of a chord sound one after another.
The notes may rise, fall or follow another pattern.
An arpeggio is an ordered, flowing type of broken chord.
It is a sequence built from chords I, IV and V in a major key.

questions.hint: The primary triads: tonic, subdominant and dominant.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a sequence built from chords I, IV and V in a major key” describes Chord progressions - chords I, IV and V in major keys, not Broken chord / arpeggio. The notes of a chord are played one after another, not together.

questions.question: The music moves from one key to another. What is this?
questions.hint: The tonal home moves somewhere new.
questions.explanation: The music moves from one key to another.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Change of key?

answers.text: The music returns to the earlier tempo after a temporary speed change.
The music moves from one key or tonal centre to another.
The chord relationships change with the new key.

questions.hint: Return to the original speed.
questions.explanation: The statement “The music returns to the earlier tempo after a temporary speed change” describes A tempo, not Change of key. The music moves from one key to another.

questions.concept: Chord progressions - chords I, IV and V in major keys
questions.question: A sequence built from the tonic, subdominant and dominant triads of a major key. What is this?

answers.text: Chord progressions - chords I, IV and V in major keys

questions.hint: The primary triads: tonic, subdominant and dominant.
questions.explanation: A sequence built from the tonic, subdominant and dominant triads of a major key.

questions.concept: Chord progressions - chords I, IV and V in major keys
questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Chord progressions - chords I, IV and V in major keys?

answers.text: It uses the tonic, subdominant and dominant chords of a major key.
It is a five-note scale using scale degrees 1, 2, 3, 5 and 6.
The chords are built on scale degrees 1, 4 and 5.
In C major the chords are C major, F major and G major.

questions.hint: Five notes: often 1, 2, 3, 5 and 6.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a five-note scale using scale degrees 1, 2, 3, 5 and 6” describes Pentatonic scale, not Chord progressions - chords I, IV and V in major keys. A sequence built from the tonic, subdominant and dominant triads of a major key.

questions.question: As an accompaniment, a note or fifth is sustained or repeated beneath a changing melody. What is this?
questions.hint: One note holds underneath changing music.
questions.explanation: As an accompaniment, a note or fifth is sustained or repeated beneath a changing melody.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Drone?

answers.text: One note, or sometimes a fifth, sounds beneath changing music.
The supporting pitch may be sustained or repeatedly sounded.
The bass pitch changes repeatedly between a note and an off-beat chord.
Bagpipe drone pipes provide a familiar example.

questions.hint: Bass on the pulse, chord on the off-beat—repeated.
questions.explanation: The statement “The bass pitch changes repeatedly between a note and an off-beat chord” describes Vamp, not Drone. As an accompaniment, a note or fifth is sustained or repeated beneath a changing melody.

questions.question: Its scale pattern is tone-tone-semitone-tone-tone-tone-semitone. What is this?

answers.text: Chord progressions - chords I, IV and V in major keys

questions.hint: C–E–G rather than C–E♭–G.
questions.explanation: Its scale pattern is tone-tone-semitone-tone-tone-tone-semitone.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Major?

answers.text: It is based on a major scale and major tonic chord.
It often sounds bright or stable, but it is not always happy.
Its natural, harmonic and melodic forms alter the sixth and seventh scale degrees.

questions.hint: Its sixth and seventh can change on the way up and down.
questions.explanation: The statement “Its natural, harmonic and melodic forms alter the sixth and seventh scale degrees” describes Minor, not Major. Its scale pattern is tone-tone-semitone-tone-tone-tone-semitone.

questions.question: Natural, harmonic and melodic forms use different sixth and seventh notes. What is this?

answers.text: Chord progressions - chords I, IV and V in major keys

questions.hint: Its sixth and seventh can change on the way up and down.
questions.explanation: Natural, harmonic and melodic forms use different sixth and seventh notes.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Minor?

answers.text: It is based on a minor scale and minor tonic chord.
It can sound darker or more tense than major, but is not always sad.
Natural, harmonic and melodic forms treat the sixth and seventh differently.

questions.hint: C–E–G rather than C–E♭–G.
questions.explanation: The statement “Its scale pattern is tone-tone-semitone-tone-tone-tone-semitone” describes Major, not Minor. Natural, harmonic and melodic forms use different sixth and seventh notes.

questions.question: The interval between notes with the same letter name, eight scale degrees apart, such as C to C. What is this?
questions.hint: Eight scale degrees, yet both notes share a letter.
questions.explanation: The interval between notes with the same letter name, eight scale degrees apart, such as C to C.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Octave?

answers.text: The notes have the same letter name.
It is a scale containing five different notes within the octave.
They are eight scale degrees apart.
The upper note vibrates at twice the lower note's frequency.

questions.hint: Five notes: often 1, 2, 3, 5 and 6.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a scale containing five different notes within the octave” describes Pentatonic scale, not Octave. The interval between notes with the same letter name, eight scale degrees apart, such as C to C.

questions.question: A decorative note or group of notes added to a melody. What is this?
questions.hint: A small decoration around the main melody note.
questions.explanation: A decorative note or group of notes added to a melody.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Ornament?

answers.text: It is a decorative note or group of notes added to a melody.
It adds expression or detail without changing the tune's basic outline.
It is a complete set of notes arranged in stepwise pitch order.
Grace notes, trills, mordents and turns are examples.

questions.hint: Notes ordered step by step, upwards or downwards.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a complete set of notes arranged in stepwise pitch order” describes Scale, not Ornament. A decorative note or group of notes added to a melody.

questions.question: In harmony, a bass note is sustained or repeated while the harmony above changes. What is this?
questions.hint: A bass note stays while the chords above change.
questions.explanation: In harmony, a bass note is sustained or repeated while the harmony above changes.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Pedal?

answers.text: A note is sustained or repeated in the bass.
The harmony changes above the bass note.
The note may fit some chords and clash with others.
Chord notes sound one after another as a rising or falling figure.

questions.hint: A chord's notes flow one after another.
questions.explanation: The statement “Chord notes sound one after another as a rising or falling figure” describes Broken chord / arpeggio, not Pedal. In harmony, a bass note is sustained or repeated while the harmony above changes.

questions.question: A scale made from five different notes within the octave. What is this?
questions.hint: Five notes: often 1, 2, 3, 5 and 6.
questions.explanation: A scale made from five different notes within the octave.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Pentatonic scale?

answers.text: Its notes have the same letter name and are eight scale degrees apart.
It contains five different notes within the octave.
It is common in Scottish, Celtic, folk and world music.
A common major form uses scale degrees 1, 2, 3, 5 and 6.

questions.hint: Eight scale degrees, yet both notes share a letter.
questions.explanation: The statement “Its notes have the same letter name and are eight scale degrees apart” describes Octave, not Pentatonic scale. A scale made from five different notes within the octave.

questions.question: A set of notes arranged in ascending or descending stepwise order. What is this?
questions.hint: Notes ordered step by step, upwards or downwards.
questions.explanation: A set of notes arranged in ascending or descending stepwise order.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Scale?

answers.text: It is a set of notes arranged in pitch order.
It is a decorative note or flourish added to an existing melody.
Its notes move stepwise upwards or downwards.
It provides pitch material for melodies and chords.

questions.hint: A small decoration around the main melody note.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a decorative note or flourish added to an existing melody” describes Ornament, not Scale. A set of notes arranged in ascending or descending stepwise order.

questions.question: Improvised jazz singing that uses nonsense syllables and sounds instead of words. What is this?
questions.hint: Improvised jazz vocals using nonsense syllables.
questions.explanation: Improvised jazz singing that uses nonsense syllables and sounds instead of words.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Scat singing?

answers.text: Nonsense syllables and vocal sounds replace normal lyrics.
Rhyming words are spoken rhythmically in time with a beat.
The singer invents ideas and may imitate instruments.

questions.hint: Rhyming words spoken rhythmically over a groove.
questions.explanation: The statement “Rhyming words are spoken rhythmically in time with a beat” describes Rapping, not Scat singing. Improvised jazz singing that uses nonsense syllables and sounds instead of words.

questions.question: Music is built around a central or home key. What is this?
questions.hint: The sense of a home pitch and home key.
questions.explanation: Music is built around a central or home key.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Tonality?

answers.text: Music is organised around a central key or tonic.
At this level it mainly contrasts major and minor.
Scale notes and chords create a pull between dominant and tonic.
A bass note alternates with an off-beat chord in a repeated accompaniment.

questions.hint: Bass on the pulse, chord on the off-beat—repeated.
questions.explanation: The statement “A bass note alternates with an off-beat chord in a repeated accompaniment” describes Vamp, not Tonality. Music is built around a central or home key.

questions.question: A repeated accompaniment pattern, often alternating a bass note on the beat with a chord off the beat. What is this?
questions.hint: Bass on the pulse, chord on the off-beat—repeated.
questions.explanation: A repeated accompaniment pattern, often alternating a bass note on the beat with a chord off the beat.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Vamp?

answers.text: One bass note stays or repeats while several different harmonies pass above it.
It is a repeated accompaniment pattern.
A bass note on the beat often alternates with a chord off the beat.
It is common in ragtime, popular and Scottish dance music.

questions.hint: A bass note stays while the chords above change.
questions.explanation: The statement “One bass note stays or repeats while several different harmonies pass above it” describes Pedal, not Vamp. A repeated accompaniment pattern, often alternating a bass note on the beat with a chord off the beat.

questions.question: An instruction to return to the previous or main tempo after a temporary change such as a rallentando or accelerando. What is this?
questions.hint: Return to the original speed.
questions.explanation: An instruction to return to the previous or main tempo after a temporary change such as a rallentando or accelerando.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of A tempo?

answers.text: It tells performers to return to the earlier tempo.
It tells performers to make the tempo gradually faster.
It follows a temporary speed change.
It restores the previous or main pace of the music.

questions.hint: The music gradually gathers speed.
questions.explanation: The statement “It tells performers to make the tempo gradually faster” describes Accelerando, not A tempo. An instruction to return to the previous or main tempo after a temporary change such as a rallentando or accelerando.

questions.question: The tempo gradually becomes faster. What is this?
questions.hint: The music gradually gathers speed.
questions.explanation: The tempo gradually becomes faster.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Accelerando?

answers.text: The beat speeds up over a passage.
It tells performers to return to the previous tempo after a change.
It is a gradual change rather than an immediate jump.

questions.hint: Return to the original speed.
questions.explanation: The statement “It tells performers to return to the previous tempo after a change” describes A tempo, not Accelerando. The tempo gradually becomes faster.

questions.question: One or more notes before the first full bar lead into the first strong beat of a phrase. What is this?
questions.hint: Notes arrive before the first complete bar.
questions.explanation: One or more notes before the first full bar lead into the first strong beat of a phrase.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Anacrusis?

answers.text: One or more notes come before the first complete bar.
The notes lead into the first strong beat of a phrase.
It is also called an upbeat or pickup.
A note stays or repeats in the bass while the harmony changes above it.

questions.hint: A bass note stays while the chords above change.
questions.explanation: The statement “A note stays or repeats in the bass while the harmony changes above it” describes Pedal, not Anacrusis. One or more notes before the first full bar lead into the first strong beat of a phrase.

questions.question: A moderate tempo marking meaning at a walking pace. What is this?
questions.hint: At a walking pace.
questions.explanation: A moderate tempo marking meaning at a walking pace.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Andante?

answers.text: It tells performers to make the tempo gradually faster.
It means a moderate walking pace.
It is calmer than allegro and faster than adagio.
It is often associated with about 76-108 beats per minute.

questions.hint: The music gradually gathers speed.
questions.explanation: The statement “It tells performers to make the tempo gradually faster” describes Accelerando, not Andante. A moderate tempo marking meaning at a walking pace.

questions.question: A metre in which each main beat divides naturally into three equal parts. What is this?
questions.hint: Each main pulse divides into three.
questions.explanation: A metre in which each main beat divides naturally into three equal parts.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Compound time?

answers.text: Each main beat divides into three equal parts.
Each main beat divides naturally into two equal parts.
6/8, 9/8 and 12/8 have two, three and four main beats.
Each main beat is often shown by a dotted crotchet.

questions.hint: Each main pulse divides evenly into two.
questions.explanation: The statement “Each main beat divides naturally into two equal parts” describes Simple time (2/4, 3/4 and 4/4), not Compound time. A metre in which each main beat divides naturally into three equal parts.

questions.question: A dot adds half a note's value, creating long-short or short-long patterns. What is this?
questions.hint: The dot adds half the note's value.
questions.explanation: A dot adds half a note's value, creating long-short or short-long patterns.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Dotted rhythms?

answers.text: Dotted notes create unequal long-short or short-long patterns.
A dot lengthens a note by half of its original value.
It is a three-part musical form with sections in the order A-B-A.
A dotted quaver followed by a semiquaver is a common example.

questions.hint: Opening section, contrast, then the opening returns.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a three-part musical form with sections in the order A-B-A” describes Ternary / ABA, not Dotted rhythms. A dot adds half a note's value, creating long-short or short-long patterns.

questions.question: A lively Scottish or Irish dance in compound time, often 6/8. What is this?
questions.hint: A lively 6/8 dance with two main pulses.
questions.explanation: A lively Scottish or Irish dance in compound time, often 6/8.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Jig?

answers.text: It is a lively Scottish or Irish dance.
It is often in 6/8 with two main beats per bar.
Each dotted-crotchet beat divides into three quavers.
It is a moderate Scottish dance in simple quadruple time with Scotch snaps.

questions.hint: Moderate Scottish dance packed with short-long snaps.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a moderate Scottish dance in simple quadruple time with Scotch snaps” describes Strathspey, not Jig. A lively Scottish or Irish dance in compound time, often 6/8.

questions.question: The tempo gradually becomes slower, usually over several beats or bars. What is this?
questions.hint: The tempo gradually applies the brakes.
questions.explanation: The tempo gradually becomes slower, usually over several beats or bars.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Rallentando?

answers.text: The tempo becomes gradually faster over a passage.
The slowing usually happens over several beats or bars.
It is often shortened to rall. in notation.

questions.hint: The music gradually gathers speed.
questions.explanation: The statement “The tempo becomes gradually faster over a passage” describes Accelerando, not Rallentando. The tempo gradually becomes slower, usually over several beats or bars.

questions.question: A short accented note followed by a longer note, often written as a semiquaver followed by a dotted quaver. What is this?
questions.hint: A short accented note jumps into a longer one.
questions.explanation: A short accented note followed by a longer note, often written as a semiquaver followed by a dotted quaver.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Scotch snap?

answers.text: A short accented note is followed by a longer note.
A dotted quaver is followed by a shorter semiquaver.
It is often written as a semiquaver followed by a dotted quaver.
It is strongly associated with the Scottish strathspey.

questions.hint: The dot adds half the note's value.
questions.explanation: The statement “A dotted quaver is followed by a shorter semiquaver” describes Dotted rhythms, not Scotch snap. A short accented note followed by a longer note, often written as a semiquaver followed by a dotted quaver.

questions.question: Metres with two, three or four crotchet beats in each bar, where each main beat divides naturally into two equal parts. What is this?
questions.hint: Each main pulse divides evenly into two.
questions.explanation: Metres with two, three or four crotchet beats in each bar, where each main beat divides naturally into two equal parts.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Simple time (2/4, 3/4 and 4/4)?

answers.text: Each main beat divides naturally into two equal parts.
It may have two, three or four crotchet beats in a bar.
Each main beat divides naturally into three equal parts.

questions.hint: Each main pulse divides into three.
questions.explanation: The statement “Each main beat divides naturally into three equal parts” describes Compound time, not Simple time (2/4, 3/4 and 4/4). Metres with two, three or four crotchet beats in each bar, where each main beat divides naturally into two equal parts.

questions.question: A Scottish dance in simple quadruple time, usually at a moderate tempo. What is this?
questions.hint: Moderate Scottish dance packed with short-long snaps.
questions.explanation: A Scottish dance in simple quadruple time, usually at a moderate tempo.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Strathspey?

answers.text: It is a Scottish dance in simple quadruple time.
It is usually played at a moderate tempo.
Dotted rhythms and the Scotch snap are important features.
It is a lively dance in compound 6/8 with two dotted-crotchet beats.

questions.hint: A lively 6/8 dance with two main pulses.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a lively dance in compound 6/8 with two dotted-crotchet beats” describes Jig, not Strathspey. A Scottish dance in simple quadruple time, usually at a moderate tempo.

questions.question: Rhythmic emphasis is placed on weak beats, off-beats or notes held across strong beats. What is this?
questions.hint: Expected strong pulses are displaced by surprising accents.
questions.explanation: Rhythmic emphasis is placed on weak beats, off-beats or notes held across strong beats.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Syncopation?

answers.text: The tempo gradually slows over several beats or bars.
Emphasis falls on weak beats or off-beats.
A note may be held across a strong beat.
The expected accent pattern is displaced, adding drive or surprise.

questions.hint: The tempo gradually applies the brakes.
questions.explanation: The statement “The tempo gradually slows over several beats or bars” describes Rallentando, not Syncopation. Rhythmic emphasis is placed on weak beats, off-beats or notes held across strong beats.

questions.question: A solo section in a concerto that lets the performer show musical and technical skill. What is this?
questions.hint: The soloist's showpiece near a movement's end.
questions.explanation: A solo section in a concerto that lets the performer show musical and technical skill.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Cadenza?

answers.text: It is a prominent solo passage in a concerto or similar work.
It is a contrasting popular-song section traditionally about eight bars long.
It displays the performer's expressive and technical skill.
It often appears near a movement's end and may be unaccompanied.

questions.hint: A contrasting pop-song section, traditionally eight bars.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a contrasting popular-song section traditionally about eight bars long” describes Middle 8, not Cadenza. A solo section in a concerto that lets the performer show musical and technical skill.

questions.question: A strict type of imitation in which one part begins a melody and another enters later with the same melody. What is this?
questions.hint: An exact melody chase, starting at different times.
questions.explanation: A strict type of imitation in which one part begins a melody and another enters later with the same melody.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Canon?

answers.text: One part begins a melody and another enters later with the same melody.
The parts continue together so the melody overlaps.
The copied musical idea may be altered and does not need to continue throughout.
Following parts reproduce the melody exactly.

questions.hint: One part copies an idea shortly after another.
questions.explanation: The statement “The copied musical idea may be altered and does not need to continue throughout” describes Imitation, not Canon. A strict type of imitation in which one part begins a melody and another enters later with the same melody.

questions.question: A musical idea in one part is copied shortly afterwards by another part. What is this?
questions.hint: One part copies an idea shortly after another.
questions.explanation: A musical idea in one part is copied shortly afterwards by another part.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Imitation?

answers.text: A musical idea is copied shortly afterwards by another part.
The copy may be exact or altered.
The copying does not need to continue for the whole piece.
Every following part must reproduce the complete melody exactly as it overlaps.

questions.hint: An exact melody chase, starting at different times.
questions.explanation: The statement “Every following part must reproduce the complete melody exactly as it overlaps” describes Canon, not Imitation. A musical idea in one part is copied shortly afterwards by another part.

questions.question: A contrasting section in a popular song, often occurring after repeated verses or choruses. What is this?
questions.hint: A contrasting pop-song section, traditionally eight bars.
questions.explanation: A contrasting section in a popular song, often occurring after repeated verses or choruses.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Middle 8?

answers.text: It is a solo concerto passage designed to display a performer's skill.
It is a contrasting section in a popular song.
It often follows repeated verses or choruses.
Its name is used even when it is not exactly eight bars long.

questions.hint: The soloist's showpiece near a movement's end.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a solo concerto passage designed to display a performer's skill” describes Cadenza, not Middle 8. A contrasting section in a popular song, often occurring after repeated verses or choruses.

questions.question: A three-part form consisting of an opening A section, a contrasting B section and a return of the A section. What is this?
questions.hint: Opening section, contrast, then the opening returns.
questions.explanation: A three-part form consisting of an opening A section, a contrasting B section and a return of the A section.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Ternary / ABA?

answers.text: It has three sections in the order A-B-A.
Verses change their words while a chorus returns with the same words and music.
The middle B section contrasts with the opening.
The final A returns and may be exact or slightly changed.

questions.hint: Changing lyrics alternate with the same returning hook.
questions.explanation: The statement “Verses change their words while a chorus returns with the same words and music” describes Verse and chorus, not Ternary / ABA. A three-part form consisting of an opening A section, a contrasting B section and a return of the A section.

questions.question: A main melody or musical idea is stated and then returns several times in altered forms. What is this?
questions.hint: One tune returns repeatedly, changed each time.
questions.explanation: A main melody or musical idea is stated and then returns several times in altered forms.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Theme and variation?

answers.text: A main melody or idea is stated first.
The idea returns several times in changed forms.
The structure is always an opening A, a contrasting B and an unchanged final A.
Rhythm, harmony, texture, tempo or instruments may change.

questions.hint: Opening section, contrast, then the opening returns.
questions.explanation: The statement “The structure is always an opening A, a contrasting B and an unchanged final A” describes Ternary / ABA, not Theme and variation. A main melody or musical idea is stated and then returns several times in altered forms.

questions.question: Verses repeat the same music with new words. A contrasting chorus returns with the same or similar words and music. What is this?
questions.hint: Changing lyrics alternate with the same returning hook.
questions.explanation: Verses repeat the same music with new words. A contrasting chorus returns with the same or similar words and music.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Verse and chorus?

answers.text: Verses repeat the same music with changing words.
A contrasting chorus returns with the same or similar words and music.
The chorus usually contains the main hook.
The complete form is always an opening A, contrasting B and returning A section.

questions.hint: Opening section, contrast, then the opening returns.
questions.explanation: The statement “The complete form is always an opening A, contrasting B and returning A section” describes Ternary / ABA, not Verse and chorus. Verses repeat the same music with new words. A contrasting chorus returns with the same or similar words and music.

questions.question: The lowest standard female voice or choral part, lying below soprano. What is this?
questions.hint: The A in SATB, second from the top.
questions.explanation: The lowest standard female voice or choral part, lying below soprano.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Alto?

answers.text: It is the highest standard female voice and carries the top SATB line.
It is the lowest standard female voice or choral part.
It forms the A in SATB.

questions.hint: The highest female part and the S in SATB.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is the highest standard female voice and carries the top SATB line” describes Soprano, not Alto. The lowest standard female voice or choral part, lying below soprano.

questions.question: One or more singers support the lead vocalist, often by adding harmony, repeated responses or sustained notes in the background. What is this?
questions.hint: Singers add harmony behind the lead performer.
questions.explanation: One or more singers support the lead vocalist, often by adding harmony, repeated responses or sustained notes in the background.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Backing vocals?

answers.text: One or more singers support the lead vocalist.
The singers form a Scottish Gaelic style that is usually unaccompanied.
They may add harmony or repeated responses.
They may sing sustained notes in the background.

questions.hint: Dance rhythms sung with Gaelic or nonsense syllables.
questions.explanation: The statement “The singers form a Scottish Gaelic style that is usually unaccompanied” describes Mouth music, not Backing vocals. One or more singers support the lead vocalist, often by adding harmony, repeated responses or sustained notes in the background.

questions.question: As a male voice, it is the lowest standard adult voice or choral part. What is this?
questions.hint: The B at the bottom of SATB.
questions.explanation: As a male voice, it is the lowest standard adult voice or choral part.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Bass?

answers.text: It is the lowest standard adult male voice.
It provides the lowest vocal line in SATB writing.
It is a high adult male voice that forms the T in SATB.
Its line often supports the harmony.

questions.hint: The T in SATB: a high adult male part.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a high adult male voice that forms the T in SATB” describes Tenor, not Bass. As a male voice, it is the lowest standard adult voice or choral part.

questions.question: A large, low-pitched untuned drum struck with a beater. What is this?
questions.hint: The biggest drum in a kit, played by foot for a deep thump.
questions.explanation: A large, low-pitched untuned drum struck with a beater.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Bass drum?

answers.text: It is a large, low-pitched untuned drum.
It is struck with a beater and provides weight or strong accents.
In a drum kit it is operated by a foot pedal.
Metal wires beneath its lower head create a crisp rattling sound.

questions.hint: Metal wires beneath the head make a crisp rattle.
questions.explanation: The statement “Metal wires beneath its lower head create a crisp rattling sound” describes Snare drum, not Bass drum. A large, low-pitched untuned drum struck with a beater.

questions.question: A low-pitched electric string instrument, usually with four strings, played by plucking or with a plectrum. What is this?
questions.hint: Four low strings link harmony to the groove.
questions.explanation: A low-pitched electric string instrument, usually with four strings, played by plucking or with a plectrum.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Bass guitar?

answers.text: A telescoping slide changes pitch instead of valves.
It is a low-pitched electric string instrument.
It usually has four strings and is played by plucking or with a plectrum.
It supplies bass lines and usually needs amplification.

questions.hint: A telescoping slide replaces the usual brass valves.
questions.explanation: The statement “A telescoping slide changes pitch instead of valves” describes Trombone, not Bass guitar. A low-pitched electric string instrument, usually with four strings, played by plucking or with a plectrum.

questions.question: An ensemble of brass instruments and percussion, with no woodwind or strings. What is this?
questions.hint: Cornets, horns, euphoniums, trombones, tubas and percussion.
questions.explanation: An ensemble of brass instruments and percussion, with no woodwind or strings.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Brass band?

answers.text: It contains brass instruments and percussion.
Flutes, clarinets and saxophones form an important part of the ensemble.
It usually has no woodwind or orchestral strings.
Cornets, tenor horns, euphoniums, trombones and tubas are common.

questions.hint: Woodwind, brass and percussion—with no orchestral strings.
questions.explanation: The statement “Flutes, clarinets and saxophones form an important part of the ensemble” describes Wind band, not Brass band. An ensemble of brass instruments and percussion, with no woodwind or strings.

questions.question: A low string instrument larger than the viola and smaller than the double bass. What is this?
questions.hint: Held between the knees and supported by an endpin.
questions.explanation: A low string instrument larger than the viola and smaller than the double bass.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Cello?

answers.text: It is a low orchestral string instrument.
It is larger than a viola but smaller than a double bass.
It is held under the chin and is the highest standard orchestral string instrument.
It rests on an endpin between the knees and is usually bowed.

questions.hint: Smallest orchestral string instrument, held under the chin.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is held under the chin and is the highest standard orchestral string instrument” describes Violin, not Cello. A low string instrument larger than the viola and smaller than the double bass.

questions.question: A single-reed woodwind instrument with a wide range and a smooth, flexible tone. What is this?
questions.hint: A straight, usually black woodwind with one reed and silver keys.
questions.explanation: A single-reed woodwind instrument with a wide range and a smooth, flexible tone.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Clarinet?

answers.text: It is a single-reed woodwind instrument.
It has a wide range and a smooth, flexible tone.
It appears in orchestras, wind bands and jazz ensembles.
It is sounded by blowing across an embouchure hole without a reed.

questions.hint: Hold this high metal woodwind sideways and blow across a hole.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is sounded by blowing across an embouchure hole without a reed” describes Flute, not Clarinet. A single-reed woodwind instrument with a wide range and a smooth, flexible tone.

questions.question: Round metal untuned percussion instruments that may be struck together or hit with sticks or mallets. What is this?
questions.hint: Round metal plates that crash or shimmer.
questions.explanation: Round metal untuned percussion instruments that may be struck together or hit with sticks or mallets.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Cymbals?

answers.text: Metal wires beneath a drum head produce a crisp rattling sound.
They are round metal untuned percussion instruments.
They may be struck together or hit with sticks or mallets.
They can create crashes, accents, rolls and shimmering sounds.

questions.hint: Metal wires beneath the head make a crisp rattle.
questions.explanation: The statement “Metal wires beneath a drum head produce a crisp rattling sound” describes Snare drum, not Cymbals. Round metal untuned percussion instruments that may be struck together or hit with sticks or mallets.

questions.question: An electronic effect that makes an amplified sound rough or fuzzy. What is this?
questions.hint: An amplified signal is clipped into a rough, fuzzy tone.
questions.explanation: An electronic effect that makes an amplified sound rough or fuzzy.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Distortion?

answers.text: It electronically alters and clips an amplified signal.
A mute changes an instrument's tone, often making it softer or more distant.
It creates a rough, fuzzy or aggressive tone.
It is especially linked with electric guitar in rock music.

questions.hint: A device softens and colours an instrument's usual tone.
questions.explanation: The statement “A mute changes an instrument's tone, often making it softer or more distant” describes Muted, not Distortion. An electronic effect that makes an amplified sound rough or fuzzy.

questions.question: The largest and lowest-pitched member of the orchestral string family. What is this?
questions.hint: The orchestra's largest, lowest bowed string instrument.
questions.explanation: The largest and lowest-pitched member of the orchestral string family.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Double bass?

answers.text: It is the largest orchestral string instrument.
It is the lowest-pitched member of the orchestral string family.
It is the smallest and highest-pitched standard orchestral string instrument.
It may be played with a bow or by plucking.

questions.hint: Smallest orchestral string instrument, held under the chin.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is the smallest and highest-pitched standard orchestral string instrument” describes Violin, not Double bass. The largest and lowest-pitched member of the orchestral string family.

questions.question: A high woodwind, usually made of metal, played by blowing across an opening instead of through a reed. What is this?
questions.hint: Hold this high metal woodwind sideways and blow across a hole.
questions.explanation: A high woodwind, usually made of metal, played by blowing across an opening instead of through a reed.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Flute?

answers.text: It is a high woodwind instrument that is usually made of metal.
The player blows across an embouchure hole.
It makes sound without using a reed.
A single reed attached to a mouthpiece begins the sound.

questions.hint: A straight, usually black woodwind with one reed and silver keys.
questions.explanation: The statement “A single reed attached to a mouthpiece begins the sound” describes Clarinet, not Flute. A high woodwind, usually made of metal, played by blowing across an opening instead of through a reed.

questions.question: A tuned percussion instrument with metal bars arranged like a keyboard and struck with hard beaters. What is this?
questions.hint: Small metal bars with a bright, bell-like sound.
questions.explanation: A tuned percussion instrument with metal bars arranged like a keyboard and struck with hard beaters.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Glockenspiel?

answers.text: Its wooden bars create a dry, sharply defined tone.
It is a tuned percussion instrument with metal bars.
Its bars are arranged like a keyboard and struck with hard beaters.
It has a bright, high and bell-like sound.

questions.hint: Wooden keyboard bars struck for a dry, bright tone.
questions.explanation: The statement “Its wooden bars create a dry, sharply defined tone” describes Xylophone, not Glockenspiel. A tuned percussion instrument with metal bars arranged like a keyboard and struck with hard beaters.

questions.question: A stick is drawn across the ridges to create a rasping rhythmic sound. What is this?
questions.hint: Scrape a stick along a ridged hollow surface.
questions.explanation: A stick is drawn across the ridges to create a rasping rhythmic sound.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Guiro?

answers.text: It is an untuned scraper with a ridged surface.
Pairs of metal jingles sound when a frame drum is shaken or tapped.
It is traditionally made from a hollow gourd or wood.
A stick is drawn over the ridges to make a rasping sound.

questions.hint: A handheld frame with pairs of metal jingles.
questions.explanation: The statement “Pairs of metal jingles sound when a frame drum is shaken or tapped” describes Tambourine, not Guiro. A stick is drawn across the ridges to create a rasping rhythmic sound.

questions.question: A large plucked string instrument with strings of different lengths stretched across a frame. What is this?
questions.hint: Forty-seven strings and seven pedals in a triangular frame.
questions.explanation: A large plucked string instrument with strings of different lengths stretched across a frame.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Harp?

answers.text: It is a large plucked string instrument with strings of different lengths.
A full-size orchestral example usually has pedals that alter its strings.
Its strings are plucked mechanically whenever keyboard keys are pressed.
It can play chords, arpeggios and glissandi.

questions.hint: Keyboard keys mechanically pluck the strings.
questions.explanation: The statement “Its strings are plucked mechanically whenever keyboard keys are pressed” describes Harpsichord, not Harp. A large plucked string instrument with strings of different lengths stretched across a frame.

questions.question: Pressing a key plucks a string inside this keyboard instrument. What is this?
questions.hint: Keyboard keys mechanically pluck the strings.
questions.explanation: Pressing a key plucks a string inside this keyboard instrument.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Harpsichord?

answers.text: Pressing its keys makes a mechanism pluck strings.
It has a bright, crisp tone and is strongly linked with Baroque music.
It offers much less dynamic control than a piano.
Its strings are plucked directly by a player's fingers and its pedals alter pitch.

questions.hint: Forty-seven strings and seven pedals in a triangular frame.
questions.explanation: The statement “Its strings are plucked directly by a player's fingers and its pedals alter pitch” describes Harp, not Harpsichord. Pressing a key plucks a string inside this keyboard instrument.

questions.question: An instrument's normal tone becomes softer, thinner, more nasal or more distant when a device is fitted. What is this?
questions.hint: A device softens and colours an instrument's usual tone.
questions.explanation: An instrument's normal tone becomes softer, thinner, more nasal or more distant when a device is fitted.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Muted?

answers.text: An electronic signal is deliberately clipped to make a rough, fuzzy tone.
A device changes an instrument's normal tone.
The sound may become softer, thinner, more nasal or more distant.
The exact effect depends on the instrument and the device used.

questions.hint: An amplified signal is clipped into a rough, fuzzy tone.
questions.explanation: The statement “An electronic signal is deliberately clipped to make a rough, fuzzy tone” describes Distortion, not Muted. An instrument's normal tone becomes softer, thinner, more nasal or more distant when a device is fitted.

questions.question: A set of tubes of different lengths tied together. Each tube makes a different pitch. What is this?
questions.hint: Different-length tubes, blown across their open tops.
questions.explanation: A set of tubes of different lengths tied together. Each tube makes a different pitch.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Pan pipes?

answers.text: It consists of several tubes of different lengths joined together.
A whistle-like mouthpiece directs air into a single tube with finger holes.
Each tube produces a different pitch.
The player blows across the open tops of the tubes.

questions.hint: Whistle mouthpiece, finger holes, no separate reed.
questions.explanation: The statement “A whistle-like mouthpiece directs air into a single tube with finger holes” describes Recorder, not Pan pipes. A set of tubes of different lengths tied together. Each tube makes a different pitch.

questions.question: An end-blown woodwind instrument with finger holes and a whistle-like mouthpiece. What is this?
questions.hint: Whistle mouthpiece, finger holes, no separate reed.
questions.explanation: An end-blown woodwind instrument with finger holes and a whistle-like mouthpiece.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Recorder?

answers.text: It is an end-blown woodwind instrument with finger holes.
The player blows across the open tops of several joined tubes of different lengths.
Descant, treble, tenor and bass are its main sizes.

questions.hint: Different-length tubes, blown across their open tops.
questions.explanation: The statement “The player blows across the open tops of several joined tubes of different lengths” describes Pan pipes, not Recorder. An end-blown woodwind instrument with finger holes and a whistle-like mouthpiece.

questions.question: This single-reed instrument has a metal body, unlike the clarinet's wooden or plastic body. What is this?
questions.hint: Brass body, single reed, woodwind family.
questions.explanation: This single-reed instrument has a metal body, unlike the clarinet's wooden or plastic body.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Saxophone?

answers.text: It is a single-reed woodwind instrument usually made of brass.
It has a powerful, flexible tone.
It is prominent in jazz, wind bands and some popular music.
It is sounded by blowing across an embouchure hole and uses no reed.

questions.hint: Hold this high metal woodwind sideways and blow across a hole.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is sounded by blowing across an embouchure hole and uses no reed” describes Flute, not Saxophone. This single-reed instrument has a metal body, unlike the clarinet's wooden or plastic body.

questions.question: An untuned drum with metal wires under the lower skin, giving a crisp rattling sound. What is this?
questions.hint: Metal wires beneath the head make a crisp rattle.
questions.explanation: An untuned drum with metal wires under the lower skin, giving a crisp rattling sound.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Snare drum?

answers.text: It is a large low drum whose drum-kit version is played with a foot pedal.
It is an untuned drum with metal wires beneath its lower head.
The wires produce a crisp rattling sound.
It is played with sticks or brushes in orchestras, bands and drum kits.

questions.hint: The biggest drum in a kit, played by foot for a deep thump.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a large low drum whose drum-kit version is played with a foot pedal” describes Bass drum, not Snare drum. An untuned drum with metal wires under the lower skin, giving a crisp rattling sound.

questions.question: The highest standard female voice or choral part. What is this?
questions.hint: The highest female part and the S in SATB.
questions.explanation: The highest standard female voice or choral part.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Soprano?

answers.text: It is the highest standard female voice or choral part.
It is the lowest standard female voice and lies below the soprano.
It usually carries the top line in choral writing.
It forms the S in SATB.

questions.hint: The A in SATB, second from the top.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is the lowest standard female voice and lies below the soprano” describes Alto, not Soprano. The highest standard female voice or choral part.

questions.question: It may be shaken, tapped or struck by hand to produce both drum and jingling sounds. What is this?
questions.hint: A handheld frame with pairs of metal jingles.
questions.explanation: It may be shaken, tapped or struck by hand to produce both drum and jingling sounds.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Tambourine?

answers.text: It is a frame drum with pairs of metal jingles.
It may be shaken, tapped or struck by hand.
A stick scrapes a ridged gourd or wooden surface to make a rasping sound.
It is usually classed as untuned percussion.

questions.hint: Scrape a stick along a ridged hollow surface.
questions.explanation: The statement “A stick scrapes a ridged gourd or wooden surface to make a rasping sound” describes Guiro, not Tambourine. It may be shaken, tapped or struck by hand to produce both drum and jingling sounds.

questions.question: A high adult male voice or choral part, lying above baritone and bass. What is this?
questions.hint: The T in SATB: a high adult male part.
questions.explanation: A high adult male voice or choral part, lying above baritone and bass.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Tenor?

answers.text: It is a high adult male voice or choral part.
It lies above baritone and bass.
It forms the T in SATB.
It is the lowest adult male voice and supplies the bottom SATB line.

questions.hint: The B at the bottom of SATB.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is the lowest adult male voice and supplies the bottom SATB line” describes Bass, not Tenor. A high adult male voice or choral part, lying above baritone and bass.

questions.question: Large tuned drums with skins stretched over bowl-shaped bodies. What is this?
questions.hint: Several large tuned drums change pitch with foot pedals and soft mallets.
questions.explanation: Large tuned drums with skins stretched over bowl-shaped bodies.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Timpani?

answers.text: Metal wires stretched beneath the lower drum head make a rattling sound.
They are large tuned kettle drums with bowl-shaped shells.
A pedal can adjust each drum's pitch.
They can reinforce harmony as well as rhythm.

questions.hint: Metal wires beneath the head make a crisp rattle.
questions.explanation: The statement “Metal wires stretched beneath the lower drum head make a rattling sound” describes Snare drum, not Timpani. Large tuned drums with skins stretched over bowl-shaped bodies.

questions.question: A bent metal bar in an open triangular shape, suspended and struck with a metal beater. What is this?
questions.hint: An open three-sided metal bar rings brightly.
questions.explanation: A bent metal bar in an open triangular shape, suspended and struck with a metal beater.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Triangle?

answers.text: It is an open triangular shape made from a bent metal bar.
Metal bars arranged like a keyboard play definite, high pitches.
It hangs freely and is struck with a metal beater.
It produces a bright, ringing untuned sound.

questions.hint: Small metal bars with a bright, bell-like sound.
questions.explanation: The statement “Metal bars arranged like a keyboard play definite, high pitches” describes Glockenspiel, not Triangle. A bent metal bar in an open triangular shape, suspended and struck with a metal beater.

questions.question: A low-to-middle brass instrument that uses a sliding tube to change pitch. What is this?
questions.hint: A telescoping slide replaces the usual brass valves.
questions.explanation: A low-to-middle brass instrument that uses a sliding tube to change pitch.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Trombone?

answers.text: It is a low-to-middle brass instrument.
A telescoping slide usually changes its pitch.
It is the highest common orchestral brass instrument and usually has three valves.
It has a powerful tone and can make smooth glissandi.

questions.hint: Three valves and the brightest, highest common brass tone.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is the highest common orchestral brass instrument and usually has three valves” describes Trumpet, not Trombone. A low-to-middle brass instrument that uses a sliding tube to change pitch.

questions.question: The highest common orchestral brass instrument. What is this?
questions.hint: Three valves and the brightest, highest common brass tone.
questions.explanation: The highest common orchestral brass instrument.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Trumpet?

answers.text: It is the highest common orchestral brass instrument.
It uses a cup-shaped mouthpiece and usually has three valves.
It has a bright, penetrating tone.
A telescoping slide usually changes its pitch and allows smooth glissandi.

questions.hint: A telescoping slide replaces the usual brass valves.
questions.explanation: The statement “A telescoping slide usually changes its pitch and allows smooth glissandi” describes Trombone, not Trumpet. The highest common orchestral brass instrument.

questions.question: The smallest and highest-pitched standard orchestral string instrument. What is this?
questions.hint: Smallest orchestral string instrument, held under the chin.
questions.explanation: The smallest and highest-pitched standard orchestral string instrument.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Violin?

answers.text: It is a low string instrument supported by an endpin and held between the knees.
It is the smallest standard orchestral string instrument.
It is the highest-pitched standard member of its family.
It is held under the chin and usually played with a bow.

questions.hint: Held between the knees and supported by an endpin.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a low string instrument supported by an endpin and held between the knees” describes Cello, not Violin. The smallest and highest-pitched standard orchestral string instrument.

questions.question: An ensemble of woodwind, brass and percussion instruments, usually without orchestral strings. What is this?
questions.hint: Woodwind, brass and percussion—with no orchestral strings.
questions.explanation: An ensemble of woodwind, brass and percussion instruments, usually without orchestral strings.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Wind band?

answers.text: It contains woodwind, brass and percussion instruments.
It contains brass and percussion only, with no woodwind instruments.
It usually has no orchestral string section.
Flute, clarinet, saxophone, trumpet and trombone may be included.

questions.hint: Cornets, horns, euphoniums, trombones, tubas and percussion.
questions.explanation: The statement “It contains brass and percussion only, with no woodwind instruments” describes Brass band, not Wind band. An ensemble of woodwind, brass and percussion instruments, usually without orchestral strings.

questions.question: A tuned percussion instrument with wooden bars arranged like a keyboard and struck with beaters. What is this?
questions.hint: Wooden keyboard bars struck for a dry, bright tone.
questions.explanation: A tuned percussion instrument with wooden bars arranged like a keyboard and struck with beaters.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Xylophone?

answers.text: It is a tuned percussion instrument with wooden bars.
Its bars are arranged like a keyboard and struck with beaters.
Its metal bars create a very high, bell-like sound.
It has a dry, bright and sharply defined tone.

questions.hint: Small metal bars with a bright, bell-like sound.
questions.explanation: The statement “Its metal bars create a very high, bell-like sound” describes Glockenspiel, not Xylophone. A tuned percussion instrument with wooden bars arranged like a keyboard and struck with beaters.
